## Conclusion — What the Map Is For

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** The real conclusion (folds the old "what this map is good for"). Four beats — what it shows, the diagnostic, the honest caveat, the call to action. No new content; this is a landing.
:::

The map is good for one thing: locating where a disagreement actually lives. If the strange neighbors a few sections back changed how you read the fight, opponents a single crux apart and allies who share only a conclusion, then it did its job.

It also leaves you with an order of operations. When a debate stalls, don't trade p(doom) numbers. First ask whether you share a frame: the same definition of doom, the same window, the same standard for what counts as evidence. If you don't, that is the disagreement, and no evidence will close it, because what looks like an argument about danger is two people running different questions. Only once the frame is shared is it worth finding the crux, the factual bet you actually differ on, because that is the part evidence can move.

An honest caveat: the map takes a position by existing. It assumes the debate is in good faith, that decomposition is the productive move, and that these disagreements are legible to this kind of analysis — a decomposition-first, empirically-conditioned stance that a committed prior-dominant thinker may find beside the point. If you've read this far and feel it missed something essential about the structure of the problem, that, too, is worth naming — and it will be a disagreement about the frame, not the facts.

So: the next time you are stuck, stop asking for a number. Ask first whether you are even answering the same question, and then ask which crux the other person is treating as settled.

The next post in the series turns to the crux beneath more of this map than any other: whether agency, and the power-seeking that comes with it, falls out of optimization itself. (If there is no link here yet, it is not out. Come back later.)
